topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun beat down on my meticulously trimmed lawn, making the sweat prickle on my neck. When Mrs. Henderson knocked on my door, I nearly dropped the watering can. "Excuse me, Mark," she began, her voice overly cheerful, "I was wondering if I might possibly borrow a little section of your yard for some herbs? I've been meaning to start a little pot garden." My cheeks instantly felt hot, a flush creeping up my neck. I stammered, "Oh, uh, well, I... I suppose you could look, but it’s not really set up for that." I kept glancing nervously toward the fence, half-expecting someone to walk by and witness my awkward deflection. I spent the next ten minutes pointing vaguely toward the overgrown corner, my hands fiddling with the strap of my gardening gloves, desperate for the interaction to simply dissolve.

[story 2]

I was wrestling with a stubborn patch of weeds when the doorbell rang. It was Mr. Davies, my neighbor from down the street, looking unusually earnest. "Hey, Liam," he said, leaning against my mailbox. "Listen, I have this idea for a small vegetable patch, and I was hoping you might let me use a bit of your space? Just a small corner, nothing major." A knot tightened in my stomach. I immediately straightened up, trying to appear casual, but my voice came out higher than intended. "Oh, uh, I don't know, Mr. Davies. I’m actually quite busy with my own projects right now. Maybe next time?" I avoided his eyes, focusing intently on the chipped paint of the porch railing. I could feel my face growing warm, a distinct pressure behind my eyes, wishing the ground would just swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The request came via a note slipped under my door—a flimsy piece of paper that felt like a public accusation. It was from Sarah, the woman who lived next door. She asked if I could let her use a small patch of my backyard for a flower bed. Reading the request, I felt my jaw clench. I spent a solid five minutes staring at the note, my gaze darting everywhere but at the paper itself. When I finally managed to speak, my voice was tight and reedy. "Well, Sarah," I managed, forcing a strained smile that didn't reach my eyes, "that’s a big ask. I don't really have much room to spare right now." I quickly turned away, walking toward the garage, hoping the sound of my retreating footsteps would drown out the sudden, intense heat rising in my chest.
